title_content.content: Multiplex Depth for Network-Valued Data and Applications

paragraph_content.content: Maoyu Zhang<sup>a,b</sup>, Linsui Deng<sup>a,c</sup>, and Wenlin Dai<sup>d</sup>

<sup>a</sup>Center for Applied Statistics, Institute of Statistics and Big Data, Renmin University of China, Beijing, China; <sup>b</sup>Goizueta Business School, Emory University, Atlanta, GA; <sup>c</sup>School of Data Science, The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen, China; <sup>d</sup>Center for Applied Statistics, BRAIN, Institute of Statistics and Big Data, Renmin University of China, Beijing, China

The demand for analyzing network-valued data is growing in various research areas. To facilitate robust analysis of such data, we introduce a novel depth concept called multiplex depth, which generalizes Tukey’s depth for multivariate data to the realm of network data. This depth is applicable to both binary and weighted networks, enabling data ordering and outlier detection. We study the depth-related properties of multiplex depth and establish the consistency of sample depth and depth-based median estimators. Furthermore, we devise eficient computational algorithms to facilitate the practical implementation. To assess the efectiveness of our approach, we conduct a comprehensive evaluation using both simulated data and a neuroscience-derived dataset of brain networks. This evaluation takes into account crucial tasks such as data ranking, center estimation, and outlier detection, ensuring a thorough and rigorous analysis of our methodology. Supplementary materials for this article are available online.
ARTICLE HISTORY Received March 2023 Accepted February 2025
KEYWORDS Data ranking; Outlier detection; Statistical depth; Subgraph structure; Two-sample test

Network is a widely used data structure for depicting relationships among objects, comprising a collection of vertices and edges connecting the vertices. In this structure, vertices represent the objects themselves and edges represent the relationships between them. The significance of network data has garnered substantial attention due to its broad application in areas such as social networks (Liu, Patacchini, and Rainone 2017; Zhu et al. 2020; Zhang, Zhang, and Dai 2024), biological networks (Zhang and Cao 2017; Zhang et al. 2024), financial networks (Han et al. 2016; Zou et al. 2017; Accominotti, Lucena-Piquero, and Ugolini 2023), and trafic networks (Dubey and Müller 2022; Zhang et al. 2022), among others. In this article, we use the terms network and graph interchangeably.

Most studies focus on analyzing a single network, such as community detection (Khan and Niazi 2017; Abbe 2017; Zhang, He, and Wang 2022), degree distribution estimation (Hay et al. 2009), or subgraph intensity estimation (Chang, Kolaczyk, and Yao 2020). However, the focus has gradually increased on the analysis of multiple networks, also known as network-valued data, where the unit of data is a single network (Lovato et al. 2020, 2021). Most research into network-valued data focuses on population modeling (Durante, Dunson, and Vogelstein 2017; Lunagómez, Olhede, and Wolfe 2021), two-sample tests (Maugis et al. 2020; Durante and Dunson 2018; Xia and Li 2022; Hu 2019; Lovato et al. 2020, 2021), community detection (Barbillon et al. 2017; Rohe, Chatterjee, and Yu 2011; Paul and Chen 2020), and other areas (Jiang, Munger, and Bunke 2001; Han et al. 2016). The multiple network data discussed above typically consists of network samples that share the same set of vertices, which is also the scenario considered in our article.

A basic challenge in network analysis is that the conclusions can be misleading if the network-valued data are contaminated by outliers. Network outliers can be roughly classified as vertex-wise and sample-wise outliers (Hodge and Austin 2004; Zhang, Meratnia, and Havinga 2010; Gupta et al. 2014). Vertex-wise outliers are unusual vertices within a single network that can occur in various domains. Sample-wise outliers are exceptional networks that exhibit unusual patterns. For example, in patients with Alzheimer’s disease, the functional responses or patterns observed through fMRI scans difer from those seen in healthy aging individuals (Greicius et al. 2004). Many studies have focused on detecting sample-wise outliers, such as Gupta et al. (2014), who detected outliers based on network community and graph similarity, using various measurements (Papadimitriou, Dasdan, and Garcia-Molina 2010; Donnat and Holmes 2018). Mining subnetwork structures comprehensively identifies and interprets sample-wise outliers (Dang et al. 2016). Both vertex-wise and sample-wise outliers are challenging. For multiple networks considered in this article, we focus on the sample-wise outliers, which motivates us to develop a robust method with statistical depth.

Statistical depth is a method of measuring the centrality of data, or the extent of outlying data, in terms of their underlying distributions, and thus generalizes the concept of order statistics to multiple types of data, such as multivariate and functional data (Tukey 1975; Liu 1990; López-Pintado and Romo 2009), directional or spherical data (Pandolfo, Paindaveine, and Porzio 2018), matrix data (Chen, Gao, and Ren 2018; Paindaveine and Van Bever 2018), curve data (Lafaye de Micheaux, Mozharovskyi, and Vimond 2021) and set data (Cascos, Li, and Molchanov 2021). Zuo and Serfling (2000a) proposed a general definition of depth for multivariate data and investigated four key desirable depth properties. Other attempts to construct depth in network data have mainly focused on single-network cases. For example, Bertagnolli, Agostinelli, and De Domenico (2020) took a novel approach and equipped projected Tukey depth with a network-embedding metric to identify the centrality of vertices, while Raj et al. (2017) extended the band depth to path ensembles within a particular network to systematically visualize the paths. A natural question, then, is how to establish a depth function for multiple networks that combines with the network structure and extends the depth properties.

paragraph_content.content: We propose a depth notion for network-valued data, named multiplex depth. This notion extends Tukey’s depth to the domain of multiple networks, enabling eficient outlier detection and network ordering. The multiplex depth framework is applicable to all unweighted/weighted and undirected/directed networks, facilitated by eficient computational algorithms. The work marks an initial attempt to extend Tukey’s concept of data depth into the realm of multiple networks, although it acknowledges some constraints on its applicability. Our contribution is multi-fold. First, we introduce an extension of the depth concept into the realm of multiple networks, incorporating both global and local network structures. Second, we establish the properties of the proposed depth measures and demonstrate the consistency of the depth-based center with the population center. Additionally, we introduce a structure weight associated with the depth, derived from the network structure, which significantly enhances the interpretability of the ranking outcomes. Lastly, we validate the efectiveness of our approach through both simulations and a study on brain connectivity, encompassing data ranking, center estimation, and outlier detection.

As a primary step in defining depth for network data, our article considers a collection of independent network samples defined over a common set of vertices. These assumptions are well satisfied by networks from various research areas, such as bioinformatics (Zhang and Cao 2017) and neuroscience (Zhou and Li 2014; Zhang et al. 2024), while could be violated by networks from some other areas, for example social networks and economic networks, where the vertex set may vary among samples. We leave the extension of our framework to more general cases as future work.

The remainder of the article is organized as follows. Section 2 describes a generalized depth notion for networks, termed (subgraph) multiplex depth, focusing on the whole network first and then gradually shifting to subgraphs. We thoroughly investigate the edgewise multiplex depth function as an important special case and introduce the structure weights tailored for network analysis. In Section 3, we examine the theoretical properties of edgewise multiplex depth and derive the consistency between the depth-based empirical center and population center. In Section 4, we evaluate the performance of the proposed depth for unweighted and weighted networks, in terms of data ranking, center estimation, and outlier detection. In Section 5, we apply the proposed depth to analyze an electroencephalography dataset via tasks of center estimation, outlier detection and a two-sample test. Section 6 provides a discussion.

However, the variation in edge weights may result in no network being completely contained within any multiplex, especially when the size of networks is large. The gMD can then degenerate, as all networks are assigned with zero depth and thereby indistinguishable. We address this by shifting our attention from global to local structures, that is, subgraphs.

In this section, we modify the multiplex depth by accounting for commonly investigated subgraphs, such as edges, two-stars, triples, triangles, and chains (Balachandran, Kolaczyk, and Viles 2017; Maugis et al. 2020; Chang, Kolaczyk, and Yao 2020). The main steps in constructing this new depth are to: (a) select a subgraph structure of specific interest; (b) calculate gMD by treating the selected subgraph as whole graph; and (c) integrate gMD values over all subgraphs of the same structure. We establish general subgraph multiplex depth by first introducing a network restriction to a subgraph indicator.

After specifying the subgraph indicator, we obtain global multiplex depths on each isomorphic subgraph. A straightforward strategy for integrating these gMD is to treat them equally and take the average. In practice, additional information may indicate that some subgraphs are more critical than others for describing the whole sample, so assigning relative structure weights is an ideal option. A detailed discussion of the structure weights is provided in Section 2.3. Thus, we can now formally propose the subgraph multiplex depth.

Subgraph isomorphism is necessary when calculating subgraph multiplex depth, but brings a heavy computational burden. The gMD restricted to isomorphous subgraphs, for example, two overlapped triangles, may be correlated, which brings dificulties in analyzing sMD. However, if we specify a single edge as the structure of interest, the computation and the theoretical analysis will be more feasible, as we will demonstrate.

Edge is a ubiquitous type of subgraph in network analyses, and meanwhile, a simpler subgraph structure leads to a more distinguishable depth function. We therefore focus on the depth notion induced by this type of basic structure, denoted as the edgewise multiplex depth (eMD). This special case is a more intuitive soft version of gMD than those based on other substructures, as it relaxes the requirement for complete inclusion to allow partial inclusion. In the following discussion, we examine the interpretability and computational feasibility of the sample version eMD.

paragraph_content.content: This section shows that eMD satisfies the commonly desirable properties for statistical depth and derives asymptotic convergence for both depth values and the depth-induced central networks. We also establish the results for gMD in Section SV of the supplementary material. In this section, we interchangeably refer to a network with G or its adjacency matrix A, as they share the same superscript and subscript.

Theorem 1 formally defines four depth properties of the eMD. The afine invariance property has two aspects: Theorem
states that applying the same edgewise afine transformation to all networks does not afect the value of eMD; Theorem

addresses the network structure: eMD remains unchanged in the case of changing the labels of vertices. As depicted in Theorem S2, gMD possesses similar depth properties for weighted networks, where “maximality at center,” “monotonicity relative to the center,” and “vanishing at infinity” properties are extended from the modified band depth for functional data (López-Pintado and Romo 2009).

For unweighted networks, we first establish the four depth properties of the gMD in Theorem S1 under Assumption S1. Given that unweighted networks are generated by perturbing the existence of edges relative to a central network

, Assumption S1 requires the independence of edge perturbations and the specified probabilities of these perturbations, which can be satisfied by a collection of noisy networks (Chang, Kolaczyk, and Yao 2020). Note that the independence of edges is a quite common assumption in popular network models, such as the stochastic block model (Wang and Wong 1987; Zhang, Sun, and Li 2020) and the latent space model (Ma, Ma, and Yuan 2020; Hof, Raftery, and Handcock 2002). This assumption is also crucial for proving the convergence of gMD and eMD for unweighted networks (Theorems 2 and S3). Particularly, the afine invariance property

The condition of unique maximization is standard when identifying a representative element for a distribution or ensemble (Ziezold 1977; Zuo and Serfling 2000b; López-Pintado and Romo 2006), which is guaranteed by the maximality at center for weighted (Theorem 1(a)) and unweighted networks (Theorem S1(a)). In a related study for networks, Kolaczyk et al. (2020) discussed the geometric requirements for the uniqueness of the Fréchet mean corresponding to the unlabeled and undirected weighted networks. On the other hand, when

is maximized at several networks, named a central network set, the estimated center will locate into the set almost surely. In practice, we recommend identifying several, rather than a single, deepest network to comprehensively analyze the property of network ensemble; see Section 5 for details. The network structure provides flexible weights according to practical necessities. If the weights of interested structures are continuous with respect to edge weights, the depth properties and consistency still hold, according to the continuous mapping theorem.

Finally, our theoretical results can be naturally extended to sparse weighted networks with consistently existent edges. We first define a subgraph indicator as an unweighted network where an edge exists between two vertices if it consistently appears in these sparse weighted networks. The depth properties and convergence theories for weighted networks can then be adapted to sparse weighted networks by restricting them to this subgraph indicator.

We compare it to the Fréchet mean, a popular tool for data ranking and center estimation. However, the sample space for unweighted networks is discrete, so finding the conventional Fréchet mean may be computationally NP-hard. This supports the selection of the following restricted Fréchet mean defined by Ginestet (2012), as this dramatically reduces the computational complexity. The restricted Fréchet mean ensures the estimated center is an existent network, which is similar to eMD.

paragraph_content.content: Vertices with higher degrees are more influential, which inspires us to define the important modules for the two models. The important module in the PA model is the subgraph composed of the vertices with degrees greater than five and the edges connecting them in the center network

. For the blog networks, the threshold for the vertices degrees is set at 20. As shown in Figure 3, we assign deeper networks with larger ranks and compare eMD and Ham using the average ranks of the networks with or without the important modules. The results of the two models are similar and both confirm the usefulness of the structure weight. We find almost no diference between the ranking results of the two groups with the equal weight for either Ham or eMD. With the vertex weight, the ranking results reveal weak diferences for eMD and remain comparable for Ham. With the edge weight, the diferences between the two groups are significant for both methods. The networks without the important module are assigned with smaller rankings, confirming the assumption that they outlie to a greater degree. These results indicate that the edge weight is more efective than the other options in capturing the important module of interest. For other practical implementations, the weight should be adapted for the specific tasks.

We apply our method to the electroencephalography (EEG) data. This dataset is derived from an empirical investigation conducted to explore the EEG manifestations of the genetic predisposition to alcoholism (http://kdd.ics.uci.edu/datasets/eeg/ eeg.data.html). The dataset comprises 77 individuals diagnosed with alcoholism and 45 control subjects. Each participant was placed with 64 scalp electrodes, which include 61 electrode caps (ECI, Electrocap International) and 3 reference channels (Khosla, Khandnor, and Chand 2020). EEG measurements were collected for each trial. Comprehensive details about this data collection can be found in the work of Zhang et al. (1995). The same dataset has also been analyzed in Li, Kim, and Altman (2010), Hung and Wang (2013) and Zhou and Li (2014).

Following their practice, our study concentrates on computing the average values across all trials conducted under the single-stimulus condition for each individual. As a result, this yields a spatial-temporal matrix of dimensions 256×64 for each subject. To make the data compatible with our method, we initially excluded three electrodes that lacked detailed descriptions, retaining a total of 61 electrodes with specific information, as listed in Table S1 of the supplementary material. Therefore, we have a spatial-temporal matrix of dimensions
for subject l. Then, for each subject, the connectivity network is constructed by calculating a matrix of dimension
, each summarizing the connectivity between the 61 brain regions.

paragraph_content.content: The brain functional connectivity is commonly modeled using graph edges with correlation coeficients (Rubinov and Sporns 2010) as weights, which signify the strength of connectivity between vertices. While binarizing these coeficients can indicate connection existence, it may overlook crucial details and the analysis outcomes depend on the chosen threshold (Colon-Perez et al. 2016; Ahmadi, Fatemizadeh, and Nasrabad 2020). Additionally, in eMD method, using a binary data structure may result in some ties. Therefore, drawing inspiration from the key idea of eMD, we prefer to integrate edge weights into our approach if we can directly obtain weighted networks from the real data. Specifically, we determine connectivity in each individual by computing Pearson correlations between samples from a pair of regions. For subject l, we set the edge weight of the connectivity network as
\dot { e ( i , j ) } = \mathrm { c o r } ( B _ { . i } ^ { ( l ) } , B _ { . j } ^ { ( l ) } )

, which is commonly employed in existing neuroscience literature (Power et al. 2011). Finally, we obtain 122 networks classified into two groups consisting of 77 networks for the alcoholism and 45 for the control.

First, we use eMD to find the central and outlying networks for each group, to investigate the diversity within and between groups. Figure 6 plots the heatmaps of the connectivity networks of two groups, with the three largest and smallest eMDs. For both groups, the main active regions of centers are in the frontal related and parietal related regions. This is consistent with existing findings that frontal and parietal regions are working in object recognition tasks (Castelli et al. 2000; Pennick and Kana

2012). To further visualize the EEG electrode positions, two groups of vertex strength heatmaps with three maximal eMDs centers are presented in Figure S5 in the supplementary material, which further supports the findings in Figure 6.

To the contrast, the outliers in both groups are extremely active in all regions. To verify the correctness of the detected outliers, we map the two group of subjects onto the Euclidean two-dimensional space via multidimensional scaling (MDS). MDS begins with computing a dissimilarity matrix based on the input data, followed by optimization that aims to minimize the discrepancy between the pairwise dissimilarities in the original data and those in the reduced-dimensional space, so as to find a lower-dimensional representation that best preserves the original pairwise dissimilarities. We apply eMD to rank the network ensembles from two groups and visualize the ranking results of each network using MDS through the cmdscale function from R package stat as shown in Figure 7. As shown, most outliers are isolated from the bulk of the data cloud, and the remaining samples display a more reasonable pattern. The performance reveals that eMD is useful and favorable in practical applications.

For weighted networks, the strength of a vertex is defined as the sum of the connected edge weights. Its distribution is a powerful tool to characterize the network properties. paragraph_content.content: Then, we propose a two-sample rank test for network data with eMD by extending the concept of Liu and Singh (1993); see the detailed procedure in Section SIV of the supplementary material. We classify the networks of alcoholism and control as two groups and test the statistical significance of the diference between them with the proposed test. The p-value is 0.00218, suggesting that the EEG networks of alcoholism and control are significantly diferent. Further, the removal of outliers from both groups of samples is necessary to ensure the reliability and validity of the analysis. Outliers can significantly influence the test results and may lead to biased interpretations, making their exclusion essential for accurate statistical comparisons. Thus, we remove the 5% most outlying samples of each group and then re-perform the two-sample test on the remaining samples. The p-value becomes 0.00186, smaller than the previous one, which reinforces the fact that the two sample groups are statistically significantly diferent. This is in line with the results in Li, Kim, and Altman (2010).

According to Figure 6, it seems that the alcoholic group has slightly lower brain activity than the control group, which requires further analysis of the group diference. Figure 9 shows the probability density functions of vertex strength of the 90% central subjects for two group. Figure 9 indicates that the alcoholic group has lower vertex strength than the control group.

is the strength of ith vertex of the lth network. Figure 10 plots the mean vertex strengths of two groups for the 61 electrodes. It is clear that brain activity in the alcoholic group is significantly lower than in the control group, especially for the recognition task related regions (CP1 to CPZ; O1 to POZ), suggesting that alcoholism suppresses neural activity in the brain connectivity. This is in line with the results in Banerjee (2014) and Abrahao, Salinas, and Lovinger (2017).

paragraph_content.content: This study provides a robust description of a collection of networks built on common vertices. We define depth notions for network-valued data by incorporating global and local network structures. These are widely applicable to various networks (e.g., unweighted or weighted, directed or undirected). The depth values provide a central-outward ranking for a network collection, and consequently a series of rank-based description statistics, such as medians and outliers. The structure weight is included during the construction of proposed depths to further account for the structural information. In addition, we investigate their theoretical properties in terms of data depth and establish the consistency of depths and their induced medians. Fast algorithms are developed to ease practical implementation. The robustness of the new depths is evaluated through extensive simulation studies and a real example.

The proposed depths can be regarded as a nonparametric modeling of a network data ensemble. We have demonstrated their applications in several canonical tasks, such as robust center estimation, outlier detection, and a two-sample test. The new depths are also applicable to network data classification and clustering (Relión et al. 2019), change point detection for dynamic networks (Huang et al. 2020), robust community detection (Martinet et al. 2020), and link prediction (Martínez, Berzal, and Cubero 2017).

Several future research directions emerge from this work. First, the proposed depth notions work well for labeled networks, and investigating their extensions to unlabeled networks is a natural next step. Second, it is possible to extend our model framework to encompass networks from diferent sets, wherein variations in the number of vertices among observations are frequent occurrences in dynamic networks. In addition, exploring the depth properties for sparse weighted networks with inconsistently existent edges is an interesting topic. Another direction is to combine external information (e.g., vertex-level or sample-level covariates) with the network itself. Further efort is required to derive the theoretical properties for a general subgraph multiplex depth and develop an eficient computational algorithm.
